questions.q: What is an entity-component system (ECS) architecture?
options: A hierarchy of game objects with inheritance (player inherits from character)
Composition-based design where entities are containers of independent components that add behavior
A network protocol for multiplayer games
A rendering technique for 3D graphics
questions.explanation: ECS separates data (components) from behavior (systems) and composition (entities). An entity is just an ID; a Sprite component renders it, a Physics component simulates it, etc. This avoids deep inheritance hierarchies and improves flexibility.

questions.q: In a scene graph, what does a transform node represent?
options: A texture applied to a 3D model
Position, rotation, and scale of an object; child nodes inherit parent transforms
A script attached to an object
A collision boundary
questions.explanation: Transform nodes form a tree where child transforms are relative to parent transforms. Moving a parent automatically moves children, enabling hierarchies like an arm bone moving with a shoulder bone.

questions.q: What is an asset pipeline in game engines?
options: A network connection for downloading game data
The process of importing, processing, and optimizing art assets (meshes, textures, audio) from external files into engine format
A menu system for accessing in-game items
A method for compressing game files
questions.explanation: The asset pipeline converts artist-created files (e.g., .fbx, .png) into optimized engine formats, applying compression, mipmap generation, and metadata tagging so assets load efficiently at runtime.

questions.q: What does a prefab (or prefabricated object) enable in game engines?
options: Faster rendering by grouping objects into larger meshes
Reusable templates for complex objects that can be instantiated multiple times with consistent properties
A way to disable objects without deleting them
Automatic texture generation
questions.explanation: Prefabs are reusable blueprints (hierarchies of objects, components, properties) that can be stamped into scenes or instantiated at runtime, ensuring consistency and reducing duplication.

questions.q: What is the difference between a game engine and a game framework?
options: No meaningful difference; terms are synonymous
An engine provides integrated systems (physics, rendering, audio, editor); a framework is lower-level, requiring more custom implementation
A framework is heavier and slower than an engine
Engines are only for 3D; frameworks are for 2D
questions.explanation: Engines like Unity or Unreal provide complete pipelines with editors, asset tools, and integrated systems. Frameworks like Pygame or SDL are lightweight libraries requiring more manual setup but offering more control.

questions.q: What does garbage collection in a game engine do to performance?
options: Improves performance by removing unused assets
Can cause frame hitches when it pauses the engine to deallocate memory
Automatically optimizes physics calculations
Has no effect on frame rate
questions.explanation: Garbage collection (automatic memory freeing) can cause unpredictable stalls when the GC pauses execution to reclaim memory, creating frame drops. Game engines often use pooling or manual memory management to avoid this.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a physics engine integration in a game engine?
options: To render lighting realistically
To simulate gravity, collisions, and rigid body dynamics without requiring custom code
To compress network traffic in multiplayer games
To generate terrain automatically
questions.explanation: Integrated physics engines (like PhysX in Unreal or Bullet in many engines) provide collision detection, gravity, joints, and forces so developers don't rewrite physics simulation.

questions.q: In game engines, what does serialization of game objects enable?
options: Loading scenes from disk by converting objects to and from disk formats (JSON, binary)
Converting 2D graphics to 3D
Networking objects between players
Rendering objects more efficiently
questions.explanation: Serialization converts objects to portable formats for saving/loading scenes and levels. Deserialization reconstructs objects from stored data, allowing persistent game state and level editing.

questions.q: What is level streaming in large game worlds?
options: Downloading game updates during gameplay
Loading and unloading world sectors dynamically as the player moves to manage memory and draw distance
Playing game audio in a continuous stream
Compressing level files for faster storage
questions.explanation: Level streaming loads only nearby sectors (chunks) and unloads distant ones, keeping memory usage constant in open worlds. This enables massive environments without loading screens.

questions.q: What is the role of a shading system in a game engine?
options: Managing game audio volumes
Defining how pixels are calculated (color, normal maps, reflections) through programmable shaders on GPU
Controlling player character animation
Handling collision responses
questions.explanation: Shaders are programs that run on the GPU to calculate final pixel color from geometry, textures, and lighting. Modern engines expose shader graphs or languages for artists and programmers to define visual appearance.
